one_line_takeaway: A non-security RLAIF/DPO alignment paper for spoken dialogue systems: single-reward preference training improves only its targeted dimension (semantic, audio, intelligibility, or emotion), while jointly combining reward datasets yields broad but imperfectly balanced gains, with an explicit emotion-vs-semantic trade-off surfacing when all rewards are combined.
key_finding: On the turn-by-turn CoT SDS (Table 2), single-reward semantic RLAIF improved LLM-judge score from 6.18 to 6.33 (p<0.01, win rate 55.4%), cut the share of low-quality judge scores (<5) from 10.2% to 7.1% (28.5% relative reduction), reduced AutoBLEU repetition from 68.3 to 56.5, and reduced perplexity from 21.2 to 19.9. Joint-Reward-v1 (semantic+audio+intelligibility) and Joint-Reward-v2 (adds emotion) maintained comparable LLM-judge scores (6.29 and 6.33) without degrading semantic quality. On acoustic metrics (Table 3), single-reward audio-quality RLAIF raised UTMOS from 2.16 to 3.06, and single-reward intelligibility RLAIF cut WER from 6.1 to 3.3; Joint-Reward-v1 achieved UTMOS 2.85 with WER reduced further to 1.0 (best intelligibility among RLAIF variants), while Joint-Reward-v2 held UTMOS at 2.85 but WER rose slightly to 1.7 (trade-off from adding emotion preferences). On emotion (Table 4), single-reward emotion RLAIF reduced Emotion Rank from 2.29 (Multi-turn CoT E2E baseline) to 1.98, but Joint-Reward-v2 (adding semantic+audio) worsened Emotion Rank to 3.00, indicating competing objectives -- the authors attribute this to semantic LLM-judge rewards pushing toward safer, more generic (less emotionally expressive) responses. For the duplex SCoT-Response model (Table 5), RLAIF improved ROUGE-L from 19.8 to 23.1, cut perplexity from 42.3 to 25.0, and raised LLM-judge score from 5.95 to 6.00, showing the blockwise log-probability-aggregation DPO formulation transfers to duplex decoding. Overall headline finding: single-reward RLAIF selectively improves only its targeted metric, while joint multi-reward training yields broad but not uniformly optimal gains, with an observed emotion-vs-semantic trade-off in the joint setting.
core_contribution: The paper proposes the first multi-reward RLAIF (reinforcement learning from AI feedback) framework for speech-in/speech-out spoken dialogue systems (SDS), jointly optimizing four independent preference dimensions -- semantic coherence, audio quality (naturalness), intelligibility, and emotion consistency -- via Direct Preference Optimization (DPO), rather than the single utterance-level semantic reward used in prior SDS RLAIF work. To make utterance-level preference labels compatible with blockwise duplex decoding (where a duplex model commits to partial utterances before turn boundaries are known), the authors introduce a formulation that aggregates per-block log-probabilities within a single DPO objective, letting one utterance-level preference pair supervise all blocks of a blockwise response. The authors construct and release a 165.7K-pair multi-reward DPO preference dataset built from the Switchboard corpus and apply the framework to both a multi-turn Chain-of-Thought (CoT) turn-by-turn SDS and a blockwise duplex SDS (SCoT-Response).
threat_model: Not applicable -- this is not a security paper. It contains no adversary, attacker capability, or threat model of any kind; it is a speech/dialogue-systems alignment (RLAIF/DPO) paper evaluated on conversational quality metrics (semantic coherence, audio naturalness, intelligibility, emotion consistency), not on any security, safety-attack, or robustness axis.
limitations: As stated by the authors: (1) preference data is constructed entirely from automatic evaluators (LLM judge, UTMOS, WER/ASR, Emo2Vec), which may introduce bias or noise relative to true human judgments -- no human-in-the-loop preference collection was used; (2) the multi-reward formulation combines reward signals via simple dataset-level concatenation/joint sampling rather than explicitly modeling trade-offs or interactions between objectives, which the results themselves show (e.g., the emotion-vs-semantic conflict in Joint-Reward-v2) can leave performance suboptimal; (3) experiments are restricted to English conversational data (Switchboard/Eval2000); multilingual and more diverse conversational domains are left to future work. Additionally noted in the results section: RLAIF-trained models occasionally produce overly safe/generic responses that fail to advance the conversation (qualitative failure mode, not quantitatively measured beyond the LLM-judge low-score-rate proxy).

datasets_used: Switchboard (Godfrey et al., 1992) — ~300 hours of spontaneous human-human telephone conversations, used to build the 165.7K-pair multi-reward DPO training dataset (51.1K semantic, 32.0K audio quality, 61.0K intelligibility, 21.6K emotion pairs)
Eval2000 — held-out evaluation set used for all reported results, following prior work (Arora et al. 2025c,d)
quantitative_results: Turn-by-turn semantic (Table 2): Multi-turn CoT E2E baseline LLM-judge 6.18 -> RLAIF (Single-Reward) 6.33 (p<0.01, win rate 55.4%); low-score rate 10.2%->7.1%; AutoBLEU 68.3->56.5; perplexity 21.2->19.9; ROUGE-L 12.1->11.9
Joint-Reward-v1 LLM-judge 6.29 (win rate 52.6%); Joint-Reward-v2 LLM-judge 6.33 (win rate 54.4%, low-score rate 7.5%)
Audio quality (Table 3): Multi-turn CoT E2E UTMOS 2.16, WER 6.1 -> RLAIF Single-Reward UTMOS 3.06, WER 3.3; Joint-Reward-v1 UTMOS 2.85, WER 1.0; Joint-Reward-v2 UTMOS 2.85, WER 1.7
Emotion (Table 4): Multi-turn CoT E2E Emotion Rank 2.29 -> RLAIF Single-Reward 1.98; Joint-Reward-v2 3.00 (worse than baseline)
Duplex model (Table 5): SCoT-Response ROUGE-L 19.8, perplexity 42.3, LLM-judge 5.95 -> +RLAIF ROUGE-L 23.1, perplexity 25.0, LLM-judge 6.00
baselines_compared: Direct E2E (single-turn E2E SDS, Arora et al. 2025d) — prior work, not this paper's contribution
Moshi (Defossez et al. 2024) — external 7B dual-channel duplex SDS baseline
Multi-turn CoT E2E (Arora et al. 2025c) — prior work backbone used for RLAIF post-training
SCoT-Response (Arora et al. 2025c) — prior duplex backbone used for RLAIF post-training
